title: Predictably Irrational: The Hidden Forces That Shape Our Decisions
author: Dan Ariely
themes: Human irrationality is systematic and predictable, not random
The decoy effect and relativity shape consumer choices in ways people do not recognize
Arbitrary anchors determine prices and valuations more than supply and demand
The word 'FREE!' triggers an emotional response that overrides rational cost-benefit analysis
Social norms and market norms are distinct motivational systems that corrupt each other when mixed
Hot emotional states fundamentally reshape preferences and decisions
Procrastination is a predictable failure of the cool-state self to control the hot-state self
The endowment effect causes people to overvalue what they own
Expectations and placebos measurably alter sensory and physical experiences
Most people cheat only a little, and cheating is governed by moral cues rather than incentives
rationale: As the most accessible and entertaining introduction to behavioral economics ever written, Predictably Irrational is the ideal entry point for the behavioral-finance leaf. It covers the foundational concepts — anchoring, decoy effect, endowment effect, zero-price effect, social vs. market norms, hot-cold empathy gap, procrastination, expectations, and placebos — through vivid experiments that make the science stick. While Kahneman's Thinking, Fast and Slow is the field's scientific apex, Ariely's book is its popular gateway: it reaches readers who would never pick up an academic text and gives them the vocabulary and intuition to understand why financial markets, consumer behavior, and personal decisions systematically deviate from rational-actor predictions. The book's direct applications to marketing, product design, and financial decision-making make it uniquely practical for investors and business professionals.
